sit: Your coach calls a timeout with 30 seconds left and your team down by one. You're going back on after the timeout. What should be the first thing in your mind?
opts: Just play hard until someone tells you otherwise — effort wins games, so skate as hard as you can and the right plays will happen on their own out there
Know your specific job for the next 30 seconds — faceoff role, where to go, who to cover
Try to score the first time you touch the puck no matter what — you're down by one with 30 seconds left so every shot needs to go on net now
Get pumped up and skate as fast as you can — energy wins the faceoff and the battles, so get loud on the bench and bring it on the next shift

sit: You're the defenseman at the point. You see the opposing center beginning to break toward your blue line for a potential counter. Your team still has the puck. What do you do?
opts: Follow the center all the way back toward your own zone to stay matched up
Hold the blue line but cheat back slightly — be ready if the puck turns over
Ignore them completely and focus on keeping the puck in deep
Call for the puck yourself so you can take the shot from the point

sit: Your first two forecheckers won the puck battle. They passed it to you as the third forward coming in. The D is still scrambling. What do you do?
opts: Stop and hold the puck to see how the defenders start to set up
Attack the scrambled D immediately — they haven't recovered. Be the second wave.
Pass back to your own D
Skate to the corner

sit: Late in a shift, you beat a tired defenseman at center ice and you're a step ahead but they're right behind you. What's the priority?
opts: Try a complex deke to beat the goalie since the defenseman is still behind you
Shoot as quickly as possible once you're in range — don't let the D recover
Pull up and wait for a teammate
Skate slowly to catch your breath

sit: The other team has a player parked in front of your net. You're the defenseman. What's the most effective way to move them?
opts: Slash their stick to move them
Get your body low and push them away from the crease — no penalty
Ignore the player in front and only focus on clearing the puck out of the slot
Lift their stick instead

sit: Your goalie stops a shot and has the puck. One winger is coming back and available. The forecheckers are still deep. What's the goalie's best move?
opts: Freeze the puck and take the faceoff in your own zone
Pass to the winger coming back — beat the forecheck before they can set up
Rim it hard around the boards past the forecheckers
Shoot it the length of the ice toward the far end

sit: Your team is cycling. The half-wall player shoots. You're the weak-side winger. What should you do the moment the shot is taken?
opts: Stay on the weak side in case the shot goes wide immediately
Drive hard to the net — be there for the rebound on the weak side
Hold at the half-wall
Back up to the blue line

sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. You're the center taking a defensive zone faceoff. The power play wants to win the draw back to the point for a shot setup. What's your goal on this draw?
opts: Win it backward to your D
Win it forward — kick it toward the corner or boards to prevent the PP shot
It doesn't matter where the puck goes as long as you win the faceoff
Tie up the opposing center's stick right after the puck drops

sit: Your team is defending. The goalie makes a save and controls the puck. The other team has three forecheckers in your zone. Your wingers are pinned. What's the right breakout option?
opts: Goalie holds the puck and stops play for a faceoff — three forecheckers means breakout is too dangerous, so a faceoff is the safer play for your team
Goalie finds the center breaking through the middle or a winger escaping — don't give the puck to a covered player
Goalie shoots the puck hard down the ice — a long clear past their blue line gets the puck out of danger and forces them to reset fully
Goalie rims the puck around the boards behind the net — the winger on the far side can pick it up as it comes around the far corner quick

sit: Two attackers are coming at you on a 2-on-1. They both have speed. The puck carrier is on your left. What's the first thing you do?
opts: Commit to the puck carrier on your left immediately — step up hard and take away the shot because the puck carrier is the biggest threat on the rush
Back up fast enough to not get beaten by their speed, stay in the middle, and force the shooter
Race forward toward the net — get back to your crease to help your goalie on the shot coming from the puck carrier on the left side of the ice
Skate to the weak side and cover the off-wing attacker — take away the back door pass and let your goalie handle the shot from the puck carrier on left

sit: You catch a pass in the slot. You see an opening to the top corner. But you hesitate for two seconds while repositioning the puck. The goalie recovers. What should you have done?
opts: Always wait for the perfect shot after a catch — take an extra second to settle the puck and pick your spot before releasing the shot on the goalie
Released immediately on the catch — the opening was there when you first received it
Passed to an open teammate in the slot — don't shoot unless you have a perfect look, and moving the puck one more time opens a better chance
Skated in closer to the net first — get to the top of the crease where the goalie can't cover as much net before you release the shot up high

sit: Your team is in the offensive zone for a long shift. The defenders are getting tired. What should your forwards be doing to take advantage?
opts: Slow the pace down so your own forwards can save their legs
Keep cycling and creating traffic — tired defensemen make mistakes under sustained
Shoot from every spot on the ice no matter the angle or lane
Change lines right away before the tired defenders can recover their wind

sit: Your team is cycling and the slot is clogged. All four opponents are below the dots. What's the play?
opts: Keep cycling low and wait for the slot to finally clear out for a scoring chance
Pass back to the open point — four defenders below the dots means the point shot is uncontested
Shoot from the corner no matter the angle and hope for a bounce off the goalie
Dump it back in and change lines

sit: You're the last man back as two attackers come in on you. Your goalie is set. One attacker has the puck on the left. One is cutting to the net on the right. What do you do?
opts: Commit to the puck carrier on the left — step up hard and take away the shot because stopping the puck carrier removes the biggest threat on the rush
Stay in the middle — take away the pass to the cutter and force the shot. Your goalie handles the shot.
Chase the cutter to the right — take away the pass and the tip since the cutter is the biggest threat and your goalie can stop the shot from the puck carrier on the left
Back up all the way to your crease — give your goalie the best sight line and let them handle the two-on-one from the top of the crease area

sit: Your team just had a power play chance and the goalie covered the puck. Faceoff in the offensive zone. You win the draw. Two of the other team's forwards are already breaking out. What's the immediate play?
opts: Set up the power play again and let your teammates handle everything — your PP is already working so you reset in the offensive zone and run it again
Get the puck to a breaking teammate immediately — the 2-on-2 counter is better than resetting the PP
Hold the puck along the boards and regroup — let your power play catch up and set up the attack after you've re-entered the offensive zone clean
Dump the puck in deep and chase — get it behind the defense and forecheck hard since you've got fresh legs on your power play unit already

sit: You're the D at the offensive blue line. Your forward is about to lose possession to a defender. If that defender gets the puck clean, they can break with speed. Should you pinch?
opts: Yes — pinch hard and help — crash down on the puck before the defender can control it because pinching saves an offensive zone shift
No — if possession is about to be lost, pinching puts you deep with no coverage during
Go to the net front instead — crash the crease area and look for a rebound while your forward battles for the puck along the boards there
Take a quick shot from the line as a distraction — firing anything on net gets everyone looking there so your forward can win the puck clean

sit: You have the puck right in front of the opponent's net. A defender has grabbed your stick and a second is pushing you from behind. The goalie is scrambling. What do you do?
opts: Stop and call loudly for a penalty — the refs will see the stick hold and the push from behind, so let them make the call on the obvious infraction
Keep your feet moving and get your stick free — any shot or swipe on net is worth taking
Fall down hard to draw the penalty — going to the ice with two defenders on you is the easiest way to get the refs to blow the whistle right away
Pass the puck out of the slot area — find a teammate at the point or half-wall to reset the play since you're tied up by two defenders

sit: Your D picks up the puck. The left side breakout is shut down — the forechecker has cut it off. Your right side has a winger breaking free. What's the play?
opts: Force the pass to the covered left winger and hope they can win a battle
Reverse — walk behind the net and find the open right side
Rim it around the boards
Hold the puck behind the net and wait for the forechecker to leave

sit: It's late in the third period. Your team is down by one. You get the puck in the offensive zone with one minute left. Your coach is yelling to shoot. You see a teammate in a clearly better position. What do you do?
opts: Shoot right away because your coach is yelling at you to shoot
Pass to the better position — the right play is still the right play
Hold the puck and take a second to think about options
Skate toward the bench for a line change

sit: Your team has been cycling for 30 seconds. The defenders have perfectly matched your cycle. Nothing is opening. What should you do?
opts: Keep cycling — eventually the defenders will make a mistake if you're patient
Stop cycling — dump the puck deep, change lines, and come back with a new entry
Shoot from the corner
Pass the puck back to your D at the point

sit: Your team has a power play with only 10 seconds left. You're in the offensive zone at the half-wall. No clear shot lane. What's the play?
opts: Dump the puck deep into the corner and chase it with the clock winding down
Shoot from wherever you are — any shot is better than nothing with 10 seconds left
Skate the puck back out, regroup, and try to set up a clean zone entry again
Hold the puck and protect it

sit: You're a forward backchecking on a 2-on-1. Your D is in the middle taking away the pass. The shooter pulls up just inside the blue line and winds up for a slap shot. You're close. What can you still do?
opts: Keep skating because you can't help anymore — get back to the net and let your goalie handle the slap shot since you're out of position now anyway
Get your stick in the shooting lane to deflect or block — you're close enough
Dive
Call for your D to rush the shooter — yell to your defenseman to step up from the middle and challenge the shooter before the slap shot gets off

sit: Your team forechecks 2-1-2. Both D and one forward are along the boards below the dots. You're the second high forward. The D has rimmed the puck to the far winger position. Who gets it?
opts: You go — you're the highest forward so the rimmed puck is your responsibility
The near winger goes — they're in better position. You stay high and be the next pass option.
Both forwards chase it hard together so one of you wins the race to the boards
The D jumps up from below the dots to retrieve the rimmed puck

sit: You're in the slot with the puck. The goalie is square. A defender is closing. Your teammate is breaking to the back post, completely open. The goalie has no idea. You have one second. What do you do?
opts: Shoot because you're in the slot — the slot is the best shooting area on the ice, so any puck from there is always worth putting on the net
Pass to the breaking teammate — open player at the back post with goalie unaware is the highest-percentage play
Hold the puck on your stick a second — wait for the defender to commit, then make your read once the play develops in front of you
Skate behind the net first — use the net as a pick on the defender and come out the other side with the puck on your forehand

sit: A forward is coming in on a clean breakaway. You're on your line. As they cross the dots, what should you be doing?
opts: Stay deep on your goal line
Skate out to the top of the crease — cut the angle and take away net
Skate all the way out past the hash marks to challenge the shooter early
Drop into your butterfly

sit: It's a 2-on-1. Your D has taken the middle and is forcing the shot. The puck carrier is cutting wide. What do you do?
opts: Cheat over toward the open passing option
Set for the shot — your D is taking away the pass, you take the shot
Rush at the puck carrier
Come out past the top of your crease

sit: The point man is winding up for a shot and there's a screener standing right in your sightline. You can't see the puck. What do you do?
opts: Give up on the shot — you can't see it — if there's no way to track the puck just cover the low corners and hope it hits you for a lucky save
Move laterally to find a gap around the screener, call to your D to move them, and be ready for a tip
Drop into butterfly early to cover the bottom half — most screened shots go low because the shooter can't see either, so seal the ice completely
Stay completely still and trust positioning — any movement takes you out of the angle, so hold your spot and let the puck hit you on the save

sit: You make a save from the left side. You can control where the rebound goes. Where's the best place to send it?
opts: Back toward the shooter
To the right corner — opposite side from where the shot came
Straight up in the air so the whistle blows and play can stop
Anywhere in front of the net so a teammate can get to it

sit: A forechecker is coming hard around the left side. The puck is sitting behind your net. You get there first. What do you do with it?
opts: Try to carry the puck out yourself
Stop it for your left defenseman who is coming to get it — get the puck to their forehand
Shoot it the length of the ice and let your forwards chase it
Freeze it
